title: Narrow Farmhouse Table
summary: A tapered-leg farmhouse dining table with a narrower-than-standard footprint, built from stacked-board legs and a floating tabletop.
description:
The tabletop is intentionally never fastened at its center — every board attaches only at its ends, both in the top-down screw pattern and again from underneath into the tabletop supports. Solid wood expands and contracts across its width with humidity, and a center screw would be fighting that movement directly, eventually splitting the board or tearing the screw loose. Working from the middle boards outward when driving screws from underneath keeps any small gaps between boards even rather than pushed to one side.

The tapered legs are simple, straight-line cuts, not curves — starting 5-1/2" down from the top and tapering in by 1" at the bottom. Doing this on a straightedge guide rather than freehand is what keeps all four legs matching.

cutList.note: Tapered starting 5-1/2" from the top, in 1" at the bottom.
Tapered the same way as the end legs.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Cut everything to length before you taper anything. From the 1x3: four end legs at 29-1/4" and two end stretchers at 23-3/4". From the 1x4, four side legs at 29-1/4" — that is 117" of the single 120" board, so cut carefully and do not waste length squaring up. From the 1x6: two side aprons at 88" and two end aprons at 23-3/4", one of each per 10 ft board. From the 1x8, four tabletop boards at 88", one per 8 ft board with 8" over. From the 2x2, eight under-tabletop supports at 23-3/4"; four per board is 95" of a 96" board before kerf, so there is no margin for a mis-cut.

Stack the eight leg pieces and confirm every one is the same 29-1/4". With the 3/4" top on, that puts the finished table at 30".

steps.title: Taper the end legs
steps.body:
Mark each 29-1/4" 1x3 end leg 5-1/2" down from the top, then mark 1" in from the same edge at the bottom, and strike a straight line between those two marks. The taper is one straight cut, not a curve.

Clamp a straightedge to the line and run the circular saw against it rather than cutting freehand — that guide is the only thing keeping all four legs matching, and a taper that wanders is obvious on a finished table. Cut all four end legs from the same setup, then stack them together and confirm the tapers agree before moving on.

steps.title: Taper the side legs
steps.body:
Taper the four 1x4 side legs exactly the same way — 5-1/2" down from the top, in 1" at the bottom, one straight cut against the straightedge guide.

Because the 1x4 is 3-1/2" wide against the 1x3's 2-1/2", the taper removes the same 1" but leaves a wider leg face. That is intended: these pieces face the long sides of the table, where the wider face reads better.

steps.title: Assemble the legs
steps.body:
Each finished leg is one 1x3 and one 1x4 joined into an L, so the table shows a tapered face on all four sides with no visible edge grain anywhere. Watch the orientation: the 1x3 pieces face the ends of the table and the 1x4 pieces face the sides.

Glue the mating faces and keep the tapers aligned at both the top and the bottom before fastening — if the two halves start their taper at different heights, the corner reads as a mistake from across the room. Fasten with 2" finish nails or 2" screws driven from the side that will end up inside. Build all four, then stand them together on a flat surface and check they are the same height with the tapers all starting at the same point.

steps.title: Attach the side aprons
steps.body:
Fasten the two 88" 1x6 side aprons across the legs, tight up under where the tabletop will sit, with glue and 2" screws into each leg. Check each leg is square to the apron as you go by measuring from the apron's top edge down to the floor at both ends of the leg.

One decision to make here: the aprons are 88", exactly the same length as the tabletop boards, so the top will finish flush with them at the ends unless you deliberately set the legs in from the apron ends. The plan gives no leg inset, so settle it before the second apron goes on.

steps.title: Attach the end aprons and stretchers
steps.body:
Fit the two 23-3/4" 1x6 end aprons between the side aprons at each end, and the two 23-3/4" 1x3 end stretchers lower down between the legs, using the same 2" screws and glue.

With 23-3/4" end pieces between the two 3/4" side aprons, the frame comes out 25-1/4" wide. Under a top of four 7-1/4" boards — 29" — that leaves about 1-7/8" of overhang on each long side. Stand the base up and measure both diagonals across the top of the frame before anything sets.

steps.title: Attach the under-tabletop supports
steps.body:
Fasten the eight 23-3/4" 2x2 supports across the frame from side apron to side apron, flush with the aprons' top edges so the tabletop lands on the aprons and the supports together as one plane.

Spaced along the 88" run with one at each end, they fall about every 12-1/2" on centres — mark them all out before driving a single screw. Use the 2-1/2" screws here, driven through the side aprons into the support end grain.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop boards from above
steps.body:
Find the centre of the frame and set your first 88" 1x8 board off it, then work outward. Four boards at 7-1/4" makes the top 29" wide.

Fasten each board at its ends only — never through the middle. Solid pine moves across its width with humidity, and a screw in the centre of a board fights that movement until something gives, either splitting the board or tearing the screw loose. Predrill and countersink every hole.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop boards from below
steps.body:
From underneath, drive screws up through the 2x2 supports into the tabletop boards, starting with the two middle boards and working outward in both directions. Working from the middle keeps any small gap variance shared evenly instead of pushed to one edge. Predrill and countersink so the 2x2 does not split, and again keep the fasteners at the board ends, not the centres.

Corner brackets at the leg-to-apron joints add rigidity if you want them, though none are listed in the materials. Fill the nail and screw holes with wood filler, sand through the grits — the tabletop especially, since it is the surface everyone touches — and finish.

images.alt: Narrow farmhouse dining table with tapered legs
